name: La Rabassa - Juberri Trail
name_original: Naturlandia desde Juberri — circular por la Capilla Sant Romà d'Aubinyà y Refugio de Roca de Pimes
description: This difficult 15.1-mile mountain biking trail in Naturland offers challenging terrain, 3256 feet of elevation gain, and Pyrenean views.
region_intro: Mountain biking in the Pyrenees provides a diverse range of terrain across this majestic mountain range that forms a natural border between France and Spain. The region features varied landscapes, from high alpine environments and wooded plateaus to deep gorges and ancient pine forests. Riders can expect natural trails, including rocky singletrack with loose boulders and sweeping descents across grassy meadows. Best mountain bike trails in the Pyrenees The most popular mountain bike trail is Bajo Peñas (variant), a 20.3 miles (32.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 27 minutes to complete. This challenging route features significant elevation changes through varied Pyrenean terrain. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Ruta Bajo Peñas – circular desde Aínsa (ZZ-007), a difficult 15.7 miles (25.2 km) path. This circular route offers views around Aínsa, known for its incredible singletracks. Local mountain bikers also love the San Vicente Trilogy v2.0 Trail, a 15.9 miles (25.6 km) trail leading through mountainous landscapes with technical sections, often completed in about 2 hours 59 minutes. Mountain biking in the Pyrenees is defined by rocky singletrack, high alpine terrain, and ancient pine forests. The extensive network of trails offers options for all ability levels, from gentle paths to challenging downhill tracks. The routes in Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 9,800 reviews. More than 119,000 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Pyrenees's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees region offers a vast network of mountain bike trails, with over 6,700 routes available on komoot. This includes a diverse range of options, from easy paths to very challenging technical trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in the Pyrenees?
A: Mountain biking in the Pyrenees features a wide variety of terrain. You can expect natural trails ranging from rocky singletrack with loose boulders and shale to sweeping descents across grassy meadows. Routes often pass through high alpine environments, wooded plateaus, deep gorges, and ancient pine forests. The region is known for its diverse landscapes, offering everything from cross-country routes to enduro and downhill tracks.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in the Pyrenees?
A: While mountain biking is possible year-round, the ideal conditions in the Pyrenees are typically found from March to June and again from September to November. Summer can be hot, especially at lower elevations, but high-mountain trails remain accessible. Always check local weather conditions before heading out.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees offer trails suitable for all ability levels. There are over 670 easy mountain bike routes available, providing gentle, well-maintained paths perfect for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes often traverse valleys and less technical terrain.

Q: Are there challenging mountain bike routes for experienced riders in the Pyrenees?
A: Absolutely. The Pyrenees are renowned for their challenging terrain, with over 3,000 difficult mountain bike routes. These include technical trails with significant elevation changes, rocky singletrack, and exhilarating downhill sections. Areas like the Hautes-Pyrénées offer high-mountain freeride adventures, and dedicated bike parks provide advanced runs.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in the Pyrenees?
A: The mountain bike trails in the Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 9,800 reviews. More than 119,000 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore the region's varied terrain, often praising the breathtaking landscapes and the diversity of the trails.

Q: Are there circular mountain bike routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, many mountain bike routes in the Pyrenees are designed as circular trails, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. An example is the difficult 15.7-mile (25.2 km) route, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970611765/ruta-bajo-penas-circular-desde-ainsa-zz-007" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta Bajo Peñas – circular desde Aínsa (ZZ-007)&lt;/a&gt;, which offers views around Aínsa.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along mountain bike trails in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees are rich in stunning natural features. You might encounter dramatic cirques like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710757" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cirque de Gavarnie&lt;/a&gt;, home to one of Europe's highest waterfalls. Other highlights include numerous mountain torrents and crystal-clear lakes, as well as majestic peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/885205" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canigou&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/151545" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte Perdido&lt;/a&gt;. Deep gorges and unique geological formations like the Orgues d'Ille-sur-Têt also add to the scenic beauty.

Q: Are there any trails that pass by waterfalls or lakes?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees are known for their abundance of water features. Many routes will take you past mountain torrents and beautiful waterfalls, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/322882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soaso Steps Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features numerous lakes, including Lake Gaube and Lac d'Oô, which is famous for its 275-meter waterfall. While specific routes aren't listed here, exploring trails in areas known for their 'gaves' (mountain torrents) will likely lead you to these sights.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by mountain bike?
A: Beyond natural beauty, the Pyrenees also offer historical and cultural elements. You might find routes passing through abandoned villages or near ancient sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/138339" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port Old Town&lt;/a&gt; is a notable historical site in the region, offering a glimpse into the area's rich past.

Q: Are there any moderate difficulty mountain bike trails in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees cater to a wide range of skill levels, including moderate difficulty. There are over 3,000 moderate routes available, offering a good balance of challenge and accessibility. An example of a moderate trail is the 14.6-mile (23.5 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1154273296/els-escorpins" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Els Escorpins&lt;/a&gt; route, which features varied terrain and elevation changes.

Q: Can I find long-distance mountain bike routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees offer several longer mountain bike routes for those seeking extended adventures. For instance, the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e951922760/salardu-vielha-salto-del-pish-varrados-bagergue-ruta-circular-por-el-valle-de-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salardú - Vielha - Salto del Pish - Varradós - Bagergue – Ruta circular por el Valle de Arán&lt;/a&gt; is a 28.4-mile (45.7 km) circular route that takes approximately 4 hours 29 minutes to complete, offering significant elevation gain and diverse landscapes.
tips: A small stone chapel located in the hills overlooking the valley. The views of the mountains and surrounding landscape are incredible. The hermitage is located at a crossroads of the Vuelta a Andorra and the Camí de Mas.

The Coll de Peguera has an approximate distance of 13.9 km from Sant Julià de Lòria, with an average slope of 6.6%. The hardest part is in the first two kilometers. Once the climb is finished, the path can continue until Port de la Rabassa.
Free Mountain Refuge. Generally in good condition. Remember !! Care must be taken among all. Please, let's leave it better than we found it !!
An adventure park above the village of Sant Julià de Lòria and a great trailhead to explore the ridge of the mountain towards Pic Negre. Great high-altitude gravel tracks in the Pyrenees.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the La Rabassa - Juberri Trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult, making it suitable for experienced mountain bikers. It features significant elevation changes and technical sections that require good riding skills.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this mountain bike trail?
faqs.answer: The route traverses Andorra's rugged landscapes, offering a variety of terrain. You can expect technical sections, singletrack, and potentially rocky stretches. It winds through alpine meadows and forests, providing a diverse riding experience.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: As you ride, you'll pass by several notable spots. These include the historic Chapel of Sant Romà d'Auvinyà, the scenic Coll de la Peguera, and the Roca de Pimes Refuge. The trail also starts near the Naturland Bike Center La Rabassa, which is a hub for mountain biking in the area. You'll also enjoy stunning views of the Pyrenean Mountain Range and panoramic vistas across Andorra.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to mountain bike in the La Rabassa - Juberri area is typically during the warmer months, from late spring to early autumn (roughly May to October). During winter, snow can make many sections impassable. Always check local weather and trail conditions before heading out.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the La Rabassa - Juberri Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is located in the La Rabassa area, home to Naturland. A good starting point with parking facilities would be near the Naturland Bike Center La Rabassa, which offers an ideal environment for accessing the local trail network.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for mountain biking in Naturland?
faqs.answer: While the general mountain biking trails in the region may not require specific permits, Naturland is an adventure park. If you plan to use any of the specific facilities, lifts, or marked trails within the Naturland Bike Center, there may be entrance fees or passes required. It's advisable to check the official Naturland website for current regulations and pricing before your visit.

faqs.question: Is the La Rabassa - Juberri Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Andorra generally welcomes dogs on trails, but specific rules can vary. While dogs are often allowed, it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock. Be sure to pack out any waste. Check with Naturland directly if you plan to bring your dog into their specific park areas.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route shares sections with several other trails. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the [CIMA AN07] La Rabassa * Juberri (54%) and also intersects with the GRP La volta a Andorra (42%). You'll also find smaller sections overlapping with El Camí IT1TR5 El Camí Capçaler: de Salzes a Balaguer (28%) and [CIMA AN07] La Rabassa * Aixirivall (20%).

faqs.question: What should I bring for a mountain bike ride on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficult rating and the mountainous terrain, you should bring appropriate mountain biking gear, including a helmet, gloves, and protective padding. Carry plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit for your bike, a first-aid kit, and navigation tools. Layered clothing is recommended due to changing mountain weather.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, being in the Naturland area, there are facilities available. Naturland itself offers dining options. Additionally, the nearby villages of Juberri and Sant Julià de Lòria will have various cafes and restaurants where you can refuel after your ride.
